title: Glasses Prescription Explained: How to Read Every Number on Your Rx
description: Can't make sense of your glasses prescription? Here's a plain-English guide to SPH, CYL, AXIS, ADD, PD, and every other number on your eyeglass Rx.
tldr: Your glasses prescription contains numbers for sphere (SPH), cylinder (CYL), axis, and sometimes ADD power. SPH corrects nearsightedness or farsightedness, CYL/axis correct astigmatism, and ADD is the extra magnification for reading in progressive or bifocal lenses.

tldrStats.label: Main power — corrects near or farsightedness

definition.text: A glasses prescription (Rx) is a written order from an optometrist or ophthalmologist specifying the corrective lens power needed to correct a patient's refractive error. It includes values for sphere (SPH), cylinder (CYL), axis, and optionally add power (ADD) and pupillary distance (PD). Prescriptions are typically valid for one to two years in Canada.

sections.h2: What a Glasses Prescription Looks Like
sections.content:
Here's a typical prescription:

OD (Right Eye): -2.50 / -0.75 x 180
OS (Left Eye): -3.00 / -1.00 x 175
ADD: +2.00
PD: 63mm

Looks like a secret code, but each number has a specific meaning. Let's break it down piece by piece.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

sections.h2: OD and OS: Right Eye and Left Eye
sections.content:
OD stands for "oculus dexter" (Latin for right eye). OS stands for "oculus sinister" (left eye). Some prescriptions use OU (oculus uterque) when a value applies to both eyes.

Your right eye and left eye almost always have different prescriptions. The brain combines the input from both, which is why the difference usually isn't noticeable to you — but the lens maker needs exact numbers for each eye.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. The sphere value is the primary correction for how your eye focuses light. It's measured in diopters (D).

Negative numbers (e.g., -2.50): You're nearsighted (myopic). You see near objects clearly but distant objects are blurry. The more negative the number, the stronger the prescription.

Positive numbers (e.g., +1.50): You're farsighted (hyperopic). Distance vision may be okay, but near objects are blurry. Higher positive numbers mean stronger correction needed.

What the numbers mean practically:

  • -0.25 to -1.00: Mild myopia — you might not need glasses full-time
  • -1.00 to -3.00: Moderate — glasses needed for driving and distance
  • -3.00 to -6.00: Moderately high — glasses needed most of the time
  • -6.00 and beyond: High myopia — full-time glasses or contacts needed

If your SPH is listed as "Plano" or "PL," it means zero correction — your eye doesn't need sphere correction (but you might still have astigmatism).

If you have astigmatism, your prescription will include CYL and axis values. Astigmatism means your cornea or lens is shaped more like a football than a basketball — light focuses unevenly, causing blur at all distances.

CYL (Cylinder) measures how much astigmatism correction you need, in diopters. It can be negative or positive depending on the notation system your doctor uses.

Axis is a number from 1 to 180 that tells the lens maker the angle (in degrees) at which the cylindrical correction needs to be placed. Without the correct axis, the astigmatism correction would be aimed in the wrong direction.

Example: -0.75 x 180 means you need -0.75 diopters of astigmatism correction placed at the 180-degree axis (horizontal).

Mild astigmatism (under -0.75) is extremely common and may not need correction. Higher values make a more noticeable difference in visual clarity.

sections.h2: ADD Power: For Progressive and Bifocal Lenses
sections.content:
The ADD (addition) value appears on prescriptions for people who need progressive or bifocal lenses. It represents the extra magnifying power added to the bottom of the lens for reading and close-up tasks.

ADD is always a positive number, typically ranging from +0.75 to +3.00. It's the same for both eyes in most cases.

  • +0.75 to +1.25: Early presbyopia — you're just starting to need reading help
  • +1.50 to +2.00: Moderate presbyopia — the most common range
  • +2.25 to +3.00: Advanced presbyopia — strong reading correction needed

If your prescription doesn't include an ADD value, you don't need multifocal lenses — single vision will work.

PD is the distance between the centres of your two pupils, measured in millimetres. Average adult PD ranges from 54 to 74mm, with most people falling between 58 and 68mm.

PD isn't technically part of your medical prescription — it's a measurement used by the lab to position the optical centre of each lens directly in front of your pupils. If PD is off by more than a couple of millimetres, you'll experience distortion, eye strain, and headaches.

Your optometrist may or may not include PD on your written prescription. If they don't, ask for it — especially if you plan to order glasses online. Any optician can measure your PD quickly at no charge.

Some prescriptions list two PD values (e.g., 31/32) — this is the monocular PD, measuring each eye's distance from the centre of your nose. This is more precise and preferred for progressive lenses. The team at Charm Optical takes both measurements during your exam.

sections.h2: Prism: When Your Eyes Don't Track Together
sections.content:
A small percentage of prescriptions include prism correction. Prism is needed when your eyes don't align properly, causing double vision or eye strain. The prism bends light to compensate for the misalignment.

Prism is measured in prism diopters (PD or Δ) and includes a base direction (Base Up, Base Down, Base In, or Base Out) that tells the lab which way to orient the prism.

Prism prescriptions are more complex and more expensive to fill. They absolutely need professional fitting — this is not something to order online without expert guidance.

sections.h2: How Long Is a Glasses Prescription Valid?
sections.content:
In most Canadian provinces, a glasses prescription is valid for one to two years from the date of the exam. In Alberta, there's no legislated expiry period, but most optical stores follow the two-year standard, and your eyes can change within that time.

The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends annual eye exams for adults over 65, people with diabetes, and those with known eye conditions. For healthy adults aged 20-64, exams every one to two years are recommended.

If your prescription is getting close to expiring, book an eye exam at Charm Optical to get updated numbers before ordering new glasses. An accurate prescription is the foundation of comfortable vision — no amount of premium lenses or coatings can compensate for wrong numbers.

sections.h2: Your Prescription Is Yours — You Can Take It Anywhere
sections.content:
In Canada, your optometrist is legally required to give you a copy of your prescription after your exam. You can fill it at any optical store, chain, or online retailer you choose.

However, keep in mind that a prescription only tells part of the story. The measurements taken during lens fitting (fitting height for progressives, PD verification, frame wrap angle) are equally important for visual performance. That's why complex prescriptions — especially those with progressive ADD or prism — benefit from in-person fitting at a trusted optical store.

If you have questions about your prescription, bring it into Charm Optical and the team will walk you through every number. Understanding your Rx helps you make better decisions about your eyewear.

Your glasses are only as good as your prescription. An outdated or inaccurate prescription means blurry vision no matter how expensive your lenses are. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every 1-2 years for adults and annually for children, seniors, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease.

A comprehensive eye exam does far more than measure your prescription. Your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels — catching conditions like glaucoma, macular degeneration, and diabetic retinopathy early, often before you notice any symptoms. They also measure your intraocular pressure, test your peripheral vision, and assess how your eyes work together as a team.

In Alberta, a comprehensive eye exam typically costs $100-$180 if not covered by insurance. Most employer plans cover the exam fee entirely. Children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health. faqs.q: What does a negative number on my glasses prescription mean?
faqs.a: A negative sphere (SPH) value means you're nearsighted — you see close objects clearly but distant objects are blurry. The more negative the number (e.g., -4.00 vs. -1.00), the stronger the prescription and the blurrier your distance vision without glasses.

faqs.q: Is my glasses prescription the same as my contact lens prescription?
faqs.a: No. Contact lens prescriptions include additional measurements (base curve and diameter) because the lens sits directly on your eye. The power values may also differ because of the different distance from the eye. You need separate prescriptions for glasses and contacts.

faqs.q: What does 'ADD' mean on my prescription?
faqs.a: ADD (addition) is the extra magnifying power for reading, used in progressive or bifocal lenses. It's prescribed for presbyopia — the age-related difficulty focusing on close objects. ADD values typically range from +0.75 to +3.00.

faqs.q: Can I get my PD measured at Charm Optical?
faqs.a: Yes. The opticians at Charm Optical in South Edmonton measure pupillary distance as part of every glasses fitting. They take both binocular and monocular PD measurements for the most accurate lens positioning.

faqs.q: Why do I need a new prescription every two years?
faqs.a: Your eyesight can change gradually without you noticing. A biennial exam catches prescription changes, but also screens for eye health conditions like glaucoma, cataracts, and macular degeneration that develop slowly. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for healthy adults.
